title: Secure Software Development and DevSecOps Policy
content:
Secure Software Development and DevSecOps Policy v2.0 — Aligned with NIST SP 800-218 SSDF, OWASP SAMM, and DoD DevSecOps Reference Design.

1. SECURE DEVELOPMENT LIFECYCLE
1.1 All software developed internally or by contractors must follow a Secure Development Lifecycle (SDLC) incorporating security at every phase.
1.2 Security requirements must be defined during the design phase and included in user stories or requirements documents.
1.3 Threat modeling (STRIDE or PASTA methodology) must be performed for all new applications and significant feature changes.
1.4 Security architecture review is required before development begins for applications handling Confidential or Restricted data.

2. SECURE CODING STANDARDS
2.1 Developers must follow the OWASP Secure Coding Practices and language-specific secure coding guidelines (e.g., CERT C/C++, CERT Java).
2.2 The following vulnerabilities must be addressed by design (OWASP Top 10 2025):
  a) Injection flaws (SQL, LDAP, OS command, XSS) — use parameterized queries and output encoding
  b) Broken authentication — implement proper session management and credential storage
  c) Sensitive data exposure — encrypt data at rest and in transit per SEC-005
  d) Broken access control — enforce authorization checks on every request
  e) Security misconfiguration — use hardened baseline configurations
  f) Insecure deserialization — validate and sanitize all input
  g) Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) — validate and sanitize URLs
2.3 Secrets (API keys, passwords, tokens, certificates) must never be committed to source code repositories. Use environment variables or secrets management tools.
2.4 All user input must be validated on the server side. Client-side validation is supplementary, not a security control.
2.5 Error messages must not expose stack traces, internal paths, or system information to end users.

3. CODE REVIEW AND STATIC ANALYSIS
3.1 All code changes must undergo peer review by at least one developer before merging to the main branch.
3.2 Security-focused code review is required for changes to authentication, authorization, cryptography, input validation, and data access layers.
3.3 Static Application Security Testing (SAST) must be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline and run on every pull request.
3.4 SAST findings classified as Critical or High must be resolved before merge. Medium findings must be resolved within the current sprint.
3.5 SAST tool false positives must be documented and suppressed with justification, not silently ignored.

4. DEPENDENCY AND SUPPLY CHAIN SECURITY
4.1 All third-party dependencies must be tracked in a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generated automatically during build.
4.2 Software Composition Analysis (SCA) must be run on every build to detect known vulnerabilities in dependencies.
4.3 Dependencies with Critical or High CVEs must be updated within 7 days or have documented compensating controls.
4.4 Dependency sources must be limited to approved registries (enterprise Artifactory, npmjs.com, Maven Central, PyPI).
4.5 Dependency pinning or lock files must be used to prevent supply chain substitution attacks.
4.6 Private packages must use organization-scoped namespaces to prevent dependency confusion attacks.

5. CI/CD PIPELINE SECURITY
5.1 CI/CD pipelines must enforce: SAST, SCA, secrets scanning, container image scanning, and unit test execution before deployment.
5.2 Pipeline configurations must be stored as code, version-controlled, and subject to the same review process as application code.
5.3 Pipeline service accounts must follow least privilege and use short-lived credentials.
5.4 Deployment to production must require at least one approval from a designated reviewer.
5.5 All pipeline executions must be logged with immutable audit trails.

6. CONTAINER AND INFRASTRUCTURE SECURITY
6.1 Container base images must be sourced from approved, maintained registries and scanned for vulnerabilities before use.
6.2 Containers must run as non-root users with read-only file systems where feasible.
6.3 Container images must not contain development tools, package managers, or debugging utilities in production.
6.4 Infrastructure as Code (IaC) templates must be scanned for misconfigurations using tools like Checkov, tfsec, or cfn-nag.
6.5 Secrets must not be baked into container images. Use runtime secrets injection (Kubernetes Secrets, Vault sidecar).

7. DYNAMIC AND PENETRATION TESTING
7.1 Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST) must be performed on all web applications before production release and quarterly thereafter.
7.2 Penetration testing must be conducted annually for all internet-facing applications and after major architecture changes.
7.3 Penetration test findings rated Critical or High must be remediated within 30 days.
7.4 Bug bounty programs are encouraged for public-facing applications.

8. REFERENCES
- NIST SP 800-218 Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF)
- OWASP Application Security Verification Standard (ASVS) v4.0
- OWASP Top 10 2025
- DoD DevSecOps Reference Design v4.0
- CISA Secure by Design Principles
- CIS Controls v8 Control 16
